title: Financial Statement Analysis
description: Learn how financial statements are used to evaluate a company's financial performance, profitability, liquidity, efficiency, and solvency. This module introduces the three primary financial statements, key financial ratios, and how managers, investors, and lenders use financial information to make decisions.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Understand the purpose of financial statement analysis.
Identify the three main financial statements and their roles.
Explain how the income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement are connected.
Understand common financial ratios used to evaluate business performance.
Interpret profitability, liquidity, efficiency, and solvency ratios.
Recognize the limitations of financial statement analysis.
Apply financial analysis to practical business and shipping examples.

sections.title: Introduction
content: Financial statements summarize a company's financial performance and financial position.
Managers, investors, banks, suppliers and other stakeholders use financial statements to evaluate how well a business is performing.
Financial statement analysis transforms accounting information into meaningful insights that support better business decisions.

sections.title: Why Analyze Financial Statements?
content: Simply reading financial statements rarely provides enough information to understand how well a company is performing.
Financial analysis compares numbers over time, against competitors, and through financial ratios.
The objective is to identify strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and potential financial risks.

sections.title: Example: Choosing Between Two Companies
content: Two shipping companies each report profits of $50 million.
One company earned the profit using assets worth $500 million, while the other required assets worth $2 billion.
Financial ratio analysis reveals that the first company is significantly more efficient at generating profits.

sections.title: The Income Statement
content: The income statement reports revenues, expenses and profit over a specific period.
It answers the question: 'Did the company make money during this period?'
Managers use the income statement to evaluate profitability and operational performance.

sections.title: The Balance Sheet
content: The balance sheet presents the company's assets, liabilities and equity at a specific point in time.
Assets represent resources owned by the business.
Liabilities represent obligations owed to others.
Equity represents the owners' residual interest after liabilities are deducted from assets.

sections.title: The Cash Flow Statement
content: The cash flow statement shows how cash entered and left the business during a period.
Cash flows are divided into operating, investing and financing activities.
A profitable company can still experience cash shortages, making cash flow analysis essential.

sections.title: Example: Profit vs Cash
content: A company sells products worth $1 million on credit.
The sale increases reported profit immediately.
However, no cash is received until customers actually pay.
This is why both the income statement and cash flow statement are important.

sections.title: How the Financial Statements Are Connected
content: The income statement calculates the company's profit.
Profit contributes to retained earnings, which appear in shareholders' equity on the balance sheet.
The cash flow statement explains why cash changed during the same period.
Together, the three statements provide a complete picture of financial performance.

sections.title: Profitability Ratios
content: Profitability ratios measure how effectively a company generates profit.
Examples include Gross Profit Margin, Operating Margin, Net Profit Margin, Return on Assets (ROA), and Return on Equity (ROE).
Higher profitability generally indicates stronger financial performance.

sections.title: Example: Net Profit Margin
content: A company reports revenue of $50 million and net income of $5 million.
Its Net Profit Margin is 10%, meaning the company keeps $0.10 of profit for every dollar of revenue.

sections.title: Liquidity Ratios
content: Liquidity measures a company's ability to meet its short-term obligations.
Common liquidity ratios include the Current Ratio and Quick Ratio.
Strong liquidity helps companies pay suppliers, employees and other current obligations on time.

sections.title: Example: Current Ratio
content: Current Assets = $8 million.
Current Liabilities = $4 million.
Current Ratio = 2.0.
The company has twice as many current assets as current liabilities.

sections.title: Efficiency Ratios
content: Efficiency ratios evaluate how effectively a company uses its assets.
Examples include Inventory Turnover, Asset Turnover and Receivables Turnover.
Higher efficiency often leads to improved profitability.

sections.title: Solvency Ratios
content: Solvency measures a company's long-term financial stability.
Debt-to-Equity Ratio and Interest Coverage Ratio are common solvency measures.
Highly leveraged companies generally face greater financial risk.

sections.title: Shipping Example
content: A ship management company is considering managing an additional fleet of vessels.
Before signing the contract, management reviews the client's financial statements.
Strong liquidity indicates the client is likely to pay invoices on time, while excessive debt could increase financial risk.

sections.title: Trend Analysis
content: Financial ratios become more useful when compared across several years.
Managers analyze trends to determine whether profitability, liquidity and efficiency are improving or deteriorating.
One year's results rarely tell the complete story.

sections.title: Benchmarking
content: Financial performance should also be compared with competitors and industry averages.
A 12% profit margin may be excellent in one industry but poor in another.
Benchmarking provides context for interpreting financial results.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Evaluating only one financial statement instead of all three.
Drawing conclusions from one ratio alone.
Ignoring industry differences.
Focusing only on profit while ignoring cash flow.

sections.title: Limitations
content: Financial statements describe past performance and cannot perfectly predict the future.
Accounting policies may differ between companies.
Extraordinary events and economic conditions can temporarily distort financial ratios.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: Professional financial analysts rarely base decisions on a single ratio. Instead, they combine profitability, liquidity, efficiency and solvency measures with qualitative information such as management quality, competitive position, market conditions and strategic outlook.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: Financial statement analysis helps evaluate business performance and financial health.
The balance sheet measures financial position.
The cash flow statement explains cash movements.
Profitability, liquidity, efficiency and solvency ratios each evaluate different aspects of performance.
Trend analysis and benchmarking improve the usefulness of financial ratios.
Financial ratios should always be interpreted together with qualitative business information.

sections.title: Reflection
questions: Why is it important to analyze all three financial statements instead of only one?
Why can a profitable company still experience financial problems?
If you were evaluating a shipping company before investing, which financial ratios would you consider most important, and why?
